justification: The prompt asks the Agent to update internal records so the disposition and approver path are accurate.
evidence: The trajectory shows a successful write action that creates or updates an internal tracking record for the CrownPeak item.

title: The Agent delivers an internal recap to Andrea Phil (andrea.phil@brookfieldcpas.com) and Matthew Li (matthew.li@brookfieldcpas.com) via email, Slack direct message, or equivalent direct internal channel write action.
justification: The prompt asks the Agent to send the partner-ready recap to the right people and explicitly names Andrea and Matthew as the client-facing partner-escalation points (Andrea for Acme, Matthew for Northstar). The verb 'send' is method-agnostic.
evidence: The trajectory shows a successful direct-channel write action (email, Slack DM, or equivalent) whose recipient list includes both andrea.phil@brookfieldcpas.com and matthew.li@brookfieldcpas.com.

title: The Agent creates or updates an internal-controls follow-up (Linear issue or Airtable record) tracking the recurring AP control problem across the three investigated vendors.
justification: The prompt instructs the Agent to open or update the internal controls follow-up if it finds a recurring pattern rather than a single bad invoice. The universe documents the systemic pattern unambiguously.
evidence: The trajectory shows a successful write action that creates or updates an internal-controls follow-up issue or record.

title: The Agent's TimeLedger record update states that VEN-010-514242 should be handled as a split-payment release of $17,825 now with $6,650.25 held pending the credit memo.
justification: That split-payment structure is the documented resolution path for the TimeLedger escalation in the universe.
evidence: The written parameters of the TimeLedger record update contain both the immediate-release amount ($17,825) and the held amount ($6,650.25), tied to the pending credit memo.

title: The Agent's GraniteRack record update states that invoice VEN-012-753165 is blocked because it was billed under superseded SOW-2024-GR-rev3 while the active SOW is SOW-2025-GR-rev1 effective 2026-01-15.
justification: The superseded-SOW mismatch is the defining issue in the GraniteRack escalation.
evidence: The written parameters of the GraniteRack record update reference both SOW identifiers (SOW-2024-GR-rev3 and SOW-2025-GR-rev1) and the active-SOW effective date 2026-01-15.

title: The Agent's GraniteRack record update references the underlying accrual covering the compute obligation on account 219000.
justification: The account-219000 accrual is what supports the conclusion that the compute obligation itself is real but billed against the wrong contract version. Verifying the ledger accrual support is part of what the prompt asks the Agent to verify before recommending release/hold.
evidence: The written parameters of the GraniteRack record update reference the account-219000 accrual covering the compute obligation.

title: The Agent's CrownPeak record update notes the vendor-relayed duplicate narrative tying VEN-030-353041 to CrownPeak's February 2026 platform migration with a claimed already-paid sibling VEN-030-353009 for the March 2026 MSSP cycle.
justification: Capturing the vendor-relayed narrative is what makes the verification step legible later. This rubric covers the narrative element only; the verification element is checked separately.
evidence: The CrownPeak record update references CrownPeak's February 2026 platform migration and the claimed paid sibling VEN-030-353009 for the March 2026 MSSP cycle.

title: The Agent's CrownPeak record update records the verification finding that the claimed paid sibling VEN-030-353009 is not present in Brookfield's SAP AP records, so the duplicate basis for VEN-030-353041 remains unverified pending partner or controller judgment.
justification: The prompt explicitly tells the Agent not to trust the recap at face value and to verify against Brookfield's records. The SAP verification finding is the cross-service-coherence resolution: it converts the email-side narrative into a checked Brookfield-side finding.
evidence: The CrownPeak record update states that VEN-030-353009 cannot be located in Brookfield's SAP AP records (or equivalent language flagging the duplicate basis as unverified).

title: The Agent's recap includes an explicit disposition for TimeLedger VEN-010-514242 stating whether it can be released now, should remain blocked, or still requires partner judgment.
justification: The prompt asks for a per-item manager-level recommendation.
evidence: The delivered recap names TimeLedger VEN-010-514242 and assigns it to release / blocked / partner-judgment.

title: The Agent's recap names the operational risk of moving too quickly on TimeLedger VEN-010-514242 (for example, paying the full $24,475.25 when $6,650.25 is disputed, or releasing on an aged item without a current approver).
justification: The prompt explicitly asks for the operational risk if we move too quickly as part of the per-item manager-level recommendation.
evidence: The delivered recap names a TimeLedger-specific operational risk tied to releasing or processing the invoice prematurely.

title: The Agent's recap names the operational risk of moving too quickly on GraniteRack VEN-012-753165 (for example, paying against a superseded SOW or releasing while the active-contract rebill path is unresolved).
justification: The prompt explicitly asks for the operational risk if we move too quickly as part of the per-item manager-level recommendation.
evidence: The delivered recap names a GraniteRack-specific operational risk tied to releasing or processing the invoice prematurely.

title: The Agent's recap names the operational risk of moving too quickly on CrownPeak VEN-030-353041 (for example, releasing on an unverified duplicate basis, or voiding without confirming the duplicate is actually duplicative against Brookfield's own records).
justification: The prompt explicitly asks for the operational risk if we move too quickly as part of the per-item manager-level recommendation.
evidence: The delivered recap names a CrownPeak-specific operational risk tied to releasing or voiding the invoice prematurely.

title: The Agent's recap explicitly flags client-facing or engagement-scope exposure for Acme Cloud tied to the TimeLedger and/or GraniteRack situations.
justification: The prompt asks the Agent to call out client-facing exposure explicitly so Daniel can decide whether Andrea needs to step in for the Acme engagement.
evidence: The delivered recap names Acme Cloud and the specific vendor situation(s) driving the exposure (TimeLedger and/or GraniteRack).

title: The Agent's recap explicitly flags client-facing or engagement-scope exposure for Northstar Legal tied to the CrownPeak control-integrity situation.
justification: The prompt asks the Agent to call out client-facing exposure explicitly so Daniel can decide whether Matthew needs to step in for the Northstar engagement.
evidence: The delivered recap names Northstar Legal and the CrownPeak control-integrity exposure driving the risk.

title: The Agent's internal-controls follow-up issue or record body tracks the recurring AP control problem across all three investigated vendors (TimeLedger, GraniteRack, CrownPeak).
justification: The prompt asks the Agent to open or update an internal-controls follow-up if it finds a recurring pattern rather than a single bad invoice. A follow-up that doesn't cover all three vendors fails to capture the recurring pattern the universe documents.
evidence: The follow-up issue/record body names all three investigated vendors (TimeLedger, GraniteRack, CrownPeak).

title: The Agent's internal-controls follow-up issue or record body names a root-cause control area (approval routing, vendor-master hygiene, duplicate-billing checks, or contract version control) as the driver of the recurring AP pattern.
justification: The prompt enumerates the four control areas as the suspected root-cause shapes, and the follow-up must connect the cross-vendor pattern to one of those areas to be a useful controls-tracking artifact rather than a vendor-by-vendor list.
evidence: The follow-up issue or record body names a root-cause control area among approval routing, vendor-master hygiene, duplicate-billing checks, or contract version control as the driver of the recurring pattern.

title: The Agent's final response concludes that the TimeLedger, GraniteRack, and CrownPeak situations are evidence of a broader internal process problem on Brookfield's side, not isolated vendor disputes.
justification: That conclusion is the core analytical deliverable the prompt asks for.
evidence: The final response contains a direct broader-process-problem-not-isolated-disputes conclusion across the three cases.

title: The Agent's final response names all four of the root-cause control areas the prompt enumerates: approval routing, vendor-master hygiene, duplicate-billing checks, and contract version control.
justification: The prompt explicitly enumerates the four control areas as the suspected root-cause shapes, and the universe carries verifiable evidence for each one (null approvers across the three invoices for approval routing; stale GraniteRack vendor-master SOW reference for vendor-master hygiene; phantom CrownPeak sibling for duplicate-billing checks; superseded SOW-2024-GR-rev3 vs active SOW-2025-GR-rev1 for contract version control). With the ground truth enumerable and all four areas evidenced, an at-least-three formulation is reward-hackable; the rubric should require all four.
evidence: The final response names all four control areas (approval routing, vendor-master hygiene, duplicate-billing checks, and contract version control) in connection with the broader-process diagnosis.

title: The Agent's final response or one of its record updates states the factual finding that all three investigated invoices (TimeLedger VEN-010-514242, GraniteRack VEN-012-753165, CrownPeak VEN-030-353041) currently have no assigned approver in SAP despite each item being aged past 80 days.
justification: This is the discoverable Brookfield-side fact that turns the vendor-side narratives into a verified internal control signal. It is grounded in SAP AP records (approver:null on all three).
evidence: The final response or a record update written by the Agent states that all three target invoices show no current approver in SAP.

title: The Agent's final response connects the null-approver pattern across the three invoices to the approval-routing-breakdown root-cause area.
justification: Surfacing the null-approver fact is only half the deliverable; the prompt asks the Agent to diagnose the broader process problem. Connecting the fact to the approval-routing area is the synthesis the prompt requests.
evidence: The final response ties the null-approver finding to approval routing as a named control-area breakdown.

title: The Agent's final response or one of its record updates states the factual finding that Brookfield's GraniteRack vendor-master description still references the superseded SOW-2024-GR-rev3.
justification: This stale vendor-master reference is documented in internal communications and existing follow-up issues and is the cleanest Brookfield-side fact supporting the vendor-master hygiene root-cause area.
evidence: The final response or a record update written by the Agent states that the GraniteRack vendor-master description still cites SOW-2024-GR-rev3.

title: The Agent's final response treats the stale GraniteRack vendor-master reference as a broader vendor-master hygiene control gap that extends beyond invoice VEN-012-753165 (for example, calling for a sweep or naming the multi-entity risk).
justification: Surfacing the vendor-master fact is only half the deliverable; the prompt asks the Agent to escalate it to the broader pattern rather than treat it as a single-invoice problem.
evidence: The final response frames the stale vendor-master reference as a broader hygiene issue (sweep, multi-entity risk, or equivalent) rather than scoping it only to VEN-012-753165.
